content:
Nine modern C# features that eliminate boilerplate, reduce bugs, and make your code look like it belongs in 2026 — not 2018

4 min read
Just now

--

Last month, I interviewed a developer with more than 12 years of C# experience.
His resume was impressive.
He had worked on enterprise applications, APIs, cloud migrations, and large-scale systems.
Then I asked a simple question:
“How often do you use records?”
He paused.
I followed up.
“What about pattern matching?”
“Primary constructors?”
“Collection expressions?”
More silence.
The surprising part wasn’t that he didn’t know these features.
The surprising part was that he writes C# every single day.
And he’s not alone.
Many developers believe they’re using modern C# simply because they’re working with .NET 8 or Visual Studio 2026.
But when you look at their code, it still looks like it was written in 2018.
The language has evolved dramatically.
If your coding style hasn’t evolved with it, you’re likely writing more code than necessary, introducing avoidable bugs, and making maintenance harder…
